NahC0el's blog

Codeforces Round 1023 (Div 2) Editorial
By NahC0el, 11 months ago,

Hope you enjoyed the round!

2107A - LRC and VIP

Solution

When all the elements of the array are equal, the solution is trivially impossible since the gcdgcd of any subset will always be equal to a1a1.

That is infact the only NoNo case. We show a construction otherwise.

Let mx=max(a)mx=max(a). Put all the elements equal to mxmx in one set, and all the other elements in the other set. Then, the gcdgcd of the first set is mxmx while the other set will have a strictly smaller gcdgcd (because gcd(a,b)≤min(a,b)gcd(a,b)≤min(a,b))

Time complexity is O(n)O(n).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    int t; cin >> t;
    
    while (t--){
        int n; cin >> n;
        vector <int> a(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            cin >> a[i];
        }
        int mn = *min_element(a.begin(), a.end());
        int mx = *max_element(a.begin(), a.end());
        if (mn == mx){
            cout << "No\n";
            continue;
        }
        cout << "Yes\n";
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            cout << (1 + (a[i] == mx)) << " \n"[i + 1 == n];
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

2107B - Apples in Boxes

Solution

Suppose max(a)−min(a)≤kmax(a)−min(a)≤k holds, and there is at least one ai≥1ai≥1. Then, infact it is possible to take one apple while still keeping the condition of max(a)−min(a)≤kmax(a)−min(a)≤k.

Proof

We will subtract from the maximum element. Then min(a)min(a) does not change except when a1=a2=…=ana1=a2=…=an. In that special case, you can check that the move is valid as max(a)−min(a)max(a)−min(a) becomes 11.

In any other case, max(a)max(a) reduces by 00 or 11, so max(a)−min(a)max(a)−min(a) only decreases, and thus max(a)−min(a)≤kmax(a)−min(a)≤k clearly holds.

Thus, for an array with max(a)−min(a)≤kmax(a)−min(a)≤k, the only way for a player to lose is when all ai=0ai=0. But this happens exactly after the ∑(ai)∑(ai)-th turn because each turn reduces ∑(ai)∑(ai) by 11.

It may be possible that a first move itself is not possible. For example, in the case that a=[4,1],k=1a=[4,1],k=1. We should check that after subtracting the maximum element, the array has the property that max(a)−min(a)≤kmax(a)−min(a)≤k and immediately print JerryJerry otherwise.

In the other case, we can simply print TomTom when ∑(ai)∑(ai) is odd, and JerryJerry when ∑(ai)∑(ai) is even. This is because when ∑(ai)∑(ai) is odd, Tom will be the last person to make a move since he went first and the total number of turns is odd, and vice versa.

Time complexity is O(n)O(n).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    int t; cin >> t;
    
    while (t--){
        int n, k; cin >> n >> k;
        
        vector <int> a(n);
        for (auto &x : a) cin >> x;
        
        long long sum = accumulate(a.begin(), a.end(), 0LL);
        
        sort(a.begin(), a.end());
        a[n - 1]--;
        sort(a.begin(), a.end());
        
        if (a[n - 1] - a[0] > k || sum % 2 == 0){
            cout << "Jerry\n";
            continue;
        }
        cout << "Tom\n";
    }
    return 0;
}

2107C - Maximum Subarray Sum

Solution

We assume that there is at least one si=0si=0 (unfilled position). In the other case that all si=1si=1, we can easily check if the maximum subarray sum is kk or not.

Let us first figure out when the answer is impossible. Replace all ai=−INFai=−INF such that si=0si=0. If the maximum subarray sum is still >k>k, then the answer is clearly impossible.

In every other case, the answer is infact possible! All positions with si=0si=0 will be kept −INF−INF except for 11. Choose that position arbitrarily, let's call it pospos.

Solution 1 (Linear)

Let b=b= max prefix sum in the subarray [apos+1,apos+2,…,an][apos+1,apos+2,…,an]. Let c=c= max suffix sum in the subarray [a1,a2,…,apos−1][a1,a2,…,apos−1]. Here we will allow the empty prefix and suffix too.

Suppose the value of apos=xapos=x. Then, the maximum subarray sum including pospos will be x+b+cx+b+c.

And the maximum subarray not including pospos will be ≤k≤k, because thats equivalent to replacing aposapos with −INF−INF.

Thus, we can simply replace xx with k−b−ck−b−c and it will satisfy the conditions.

Solution 2 (Binary Search)

Let f(x)f(x) be defined as the maximum subarray sum when we replace aposapos with xx.

Note the following properties of f(x)f(x):

  • f(−INF)≤kf(−INF)≤k: Because this is the assumption for the non-impossible case.

  • f(k)≥kf(k)≥k: Because the subarray [apos][apos] itself has a sum of kk, so the maximum subarray sum must be greater.

  • f(x+1)≥f(x)f(x+1)≥f(x) : Because increasing an element cannot reduce the maximum subarray sum.

  • f(x+1)≤f(x)+1f(x+1)≤f(x)+1: Because by increasing aposapos, each subarray increases by 00 or 11 depending on whether it includes pospos. Thus, there is no way for the function calculating maximum subarray sum to increase by more than 11.

With these observations, we can infact binary search on the first xx such that f(x)=kf(x)=k.

This is because, ff becomes continuous on the set of integers by the third and fourth property, and hence it will take all values in the range [f(a),f(b)][f(a),f(b)] for a≤ba≤b, and we have shown that k∈[f(−INF),f(k)]k∈[f(−INF),f(k)] by the first and second property.

In both solutions, be careful to avoid overflow. One simple way is to have INF=1013INF=1013, as that is sufficient for our purposes (prevents overflow, but still larger than k+∑aik+∑ai).

Time complexity: O(n)O(n) or O(n⋅log(A))O(n⋅log⁡(A)).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--){
        int n; 
        long long k; 
        cin >> n >> k;
        string s; cin >> s;
        vector <long long> a(n);
        for (auto &x : a) cin >> x;
        
        int pos = -1;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            if (s[i] == '0'){
                pos = i;
                a[i] = -1e13;
            }
        }
        
        long long mx = 0;
        long long curr = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            curr = max(curr + a[i], a[i]);
            mx = max(mx, curr);
        }
        if (mx > k || (mx != k && pos == -1)){
            cout << "No\n";
            continue;
        }
        if (pos != -1){
            mx = 0, curr = 0;
            long long L, R;
            
            for (int i = pos + 1; i < n; i++){
                curr += a[i];
                mx = max(mx, curr);
            }
            L = mx;
            mx = 0;
            curr = 0;
            for (int i = pos - 1; i >= 0; i--){
                curr += a[i];
                mx = max(mx, curr);
            }
            R = mx;
            
            a[pos] = k - L - R;
        }
        
        cout << "Yes\n";
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            cout << a[i] << " \n"[i + 1 == n];
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

2107D - Apple Tree Traversing

Solution

Note that the problem has a simple O(n2)O(n2) solution by greedily finding the largest diameter in the current forest (set of disjoint trees) at every step, tiebreaking by the lexicographic order of the endpoints, then removing the diameter and continuing this process while at least 11 node remains.

Property 11: 22 diameters always share at least one node.

Proof

With some background theory about diameters, this becomes trivial as when diameter length (in terms of nodes) is odd, they share a common central node; and when even, they share an edge (which shares 22 nodes). Nevertheless, we provide an elementary proof.

Let a1,a2,…ada1,a2,…ad and b1,b2,…bdb1,b2,…bd be 22 distinct diameters that do not share any node.

Consider the closest pair of 22 points in these 22 paths, say aiai and bjbj are closest. Then, the path (ai,bj)(ai,bj) cannot contain any other akak or any other bkbk because otherwise it would be a contradiction to the fact that they are closest.

Now, the length of the path (a1,b1)(a1,b1) is (i−1)+dist(ai,bj)+(j−1)(i−1)+dist⁡(ai,bj)+(j−1), where dist(x,y)dist⁡(x,y) denotes the number of nodes on the path (x,y)(x,y).

Assume that i,j≥d+12i,j≥d+12, then dist(a1,b1)dist⁡(a1,b1) is strictly larger than dd (using dist(ai,bj)≥2dist⁡(ai,bj)≥2), contradicting that dd is the diameter. In the other cases of i,ji,j being smaller than d+12d+12, we can check the pairs (a1,bd),(ad,b1)(a1,bd),(ad,b1) and (ad,bd)(ad,bd). It is easy to see at least one of them will be larger than dd.

Property 22 : Let (u,v)(u,v) denote a diameter path, and TT denote the tree. Then diam(T∖(u,v))<diam(T)diam⁡(T∖(u,v))<diam⁡(T) (strictly smaller).

Proof

This comes directly from the previous property. Since all diameters share at least one node, the new forest generated after removing path (u,v)(u,v) will have a strictly smaller diameter.

Property 33 : In any sequence of positive numbers such that a1+a2+…+ak≤na1+a2+…+ak≤n such that ai<ajai<aj for all 1≤i<j≤k1≤i<j≤k, kk is at most O(n−−√)O(n).

Proof

This is a classical property.

Note that ai≥iai≥i for all 1≤i≤k1≤i≤k by induction. And thus, a1+a2+…+ak≥1+2+…+k=k⋅(k+1)2a1+a2+…+ak≥1+2+…+k=k⋅(k+1)2.

But, a1+a2+…+ak≤na1+a2+…+ak≤n implies k⋅(k+1)2≤nk⋅(k+1)2≤n, and so k≤2⋅n−−√k≤2⋅n.

With these 33 properties, we can now solve the problem. We describe the algorithm below:

  • Maintain a collection of subtrees (of nodes which have apples). Initially, the whole tree is there as one component.

  • For every subtree, find it's lexicographically largest triplet of (d,u,v)(d,u,v) using a BFS/DFS.

  • Remove the nodes on the diameter path for each subtree. This divides the component into several smaller subtrees. Add them to our collection.

  • Repeat steps 22 and 33 while our collection is non-empty.

  • Finally, sort (in descending order) the list of (d,u,v)(d,u,v) collected throughout all the steps, and output.

It is not hard to see that the above approach produces the correct answer. And it runs in O(n⋅n−−√)O(n⋅n) time because steps 22 and 33 are repeated only O(n−−√)O(n) times.

Proof

Every smaller tree is formed from a larger "parent" tree. The diameter of the smaller tree is smaller than the diameter of the larger tree using Property 22.

Suppose that there is some tree that is formed at kk-th step. Then, consider the sequence of parent trees of this tree, and their diameters. Their diameters form a strictly increasing sequence, but their sum must be bounded by nn since we remove that many nodes, and total removed nodes is nn. Thus, using Property 33, we get k≤2⋅n−−−−√k≤2⋅n.

There are several correct ways to implement Step 22. One of the neatest way is as follows: Find the furthest node from 11, tie breaking lexicographically, say we got xx. Find the furthest node from xx, again tie breaking lexicopgraphically, say we got yy. Then (max(x,y),min(x,y))(max(x,y),min(x,y)) is the required diameter. To prove that these 22 traversals are enough, you can use the fact mentioned in proof of property 11, i.e. diameters share a central node or an edge (depending on parity). To find the furthest node, we can use BFS or DFS both fairly easily.

For step 33, we can keep recursing to the parent of yy till we reach xx, assuming we have calculated the parents of all vertices with a dfs from xx. You may refer to the code.

Code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

mt19937_64 RNG(chrono::steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());

int main()
{
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--){
        int n; cin >> n;
        
        vector<vector<int>> adj(n + 1);
        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++){
            int u, v; cin >> u >> v;
            
            adj[u].push_back(v);
            adj[v].push_back(u);
        }
        
        vector <bool> used(n + 1, false), seen(n + 1, false);
        vector <array<int, 3>> ans;
        vector <int> p(n + 1, -1);
        
        while (true){
            if (count(used.begin() + 1, used.end(), false) == 0){
                break;
            }
            seen.assign(n + 1, false);
            
            auto dfs = [&](auto self, int u, int par) -> pair<int, int>{
                pair <int, int> ans = {1, u};
                p[u] = par;
                seen[u] = true;
                
                for (int v : adj[u]){
                    if (v != par && !used[v]){
                        auto pi = self(self, v, u);
                        pi.first += 1;
                        ans = max(ans, pi);
                    }
                }
                
                return ans;
            };
            
            for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) if (!used[i] && !seen[i]){
                auto [d1, j] = dfs(dfs, i, -1);
                auto [d2, k] = dfs(dfs, j, -1);
                ans.push_back({d2, max(j, k), min(j, k)});
                
                while (k != -1){
                    used[k] = true;
                    k = p[k];
                }
            }
        }
        
        sort(ans.begin(), ans.end(), greater<array<int, 3>>());
        for (auto [x, y, z] : ans){
            cout << x << " " << y << " " << z << " ";
        }
        cout << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}
Bonus 1

Solve the problem in O(n⋅log(n))O(n⋅log⁡(n)).

Solution

Let fi,gifi,gi be the longest and second longest path from ii in ii's subtree, the diameter will be maxi=1nfi+gimaxi=1nfi+gi.

When we find a diameter (u,v)(u,v), let update fu,gufu,gu for all uu on the path (lca(u,v),root)(lca⁡(u,v),root). Note that the path (lca(u,v),root)(lca⁡(u,v),root) will be always shorter than the diameter, so the total update times will be O(n)O(n).

Use a std::set to find fu,gufu,gu for every node uu and a std::priority_queue to maintain the maximum fi+gifi+gi, the total complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
#ifdef DEBUG_LOCAL
#include <mydebug/debug.h>
#endif
using ll = long long;
const int N = 5e5+5;
using namespace std;
using pi = pair<int,int>;
using ti = tuple<int,int,int,int>;
int T,n,u,v,del[N],fa[N],ct;  vector<int> g[N]; set<pi> t[N];
void dfs(int u,int f){
    t[u].emplace(0,u),fa[u] = f;
    for(int v : g[u]) if(v != f){
        dfs(v,u); auto [x,y] = *--t[v].end();
        t[u].emplace(x+1,y);
    }
}ti gt(int u){
    assert(t[u].size() >= 1);
    if(t[u].size() == 1) return {0,u,u,u};
    auto [x,y] = *--t[u].end();
    auto [p,q] = *--(--t[u].end());
    return {x + p,max(y,q),min(y,q),u};
}void los(){
    cin >> n;
    for(int i = 1;i <= n;i ++) g[i].clear(),del[i] = 0,t[i].clear();
    for(int i = 1;i < n;i ++) cin >> u >> v,g[u].push_back(v),g[v].push_back(u);
    ct = 0,dfs(1,0); priority_queue<ti> q;
    for(int i = 1;i <= n;i ++) q.emplace(gt(i));
    while(q.size()){
        auto [di,u,v,d] = q.top(); q.pop();
        if(del[d] || ti{di,u,v,d} != gt(d)) continue;
        cout << di + 1 << " " << u << " " << v << " ";
        while(u != d) del[u] = 1,u = fa[u];
        while(v != d) del[v] = 1,v = fa[v];
        del[d] = 1; 
        auto [x,y] = *--t[d].end();
        while(fa[d] && !del[fa[d]]){
            d = fa[d];
            if(t[d].count({++x,y})) t[d].erase({x,y});
            q.emplace(gt(d));
            if(fa[d]){
                auto [a,b] = *--t[d].end();
                t[fa[d]].emplace(a+1,b);
            }
        }
    }cout << "\n";
}int main(){
    ios::sync_with_stdio(0),cin.tie(0);
    for(cin >> T;T --;) los();
}
Bonus 2

Compute provably the exact worst case, and construct a tree that obtains it.

Best Bound

It can be proven that diameter decreases by 22 each iteration and not just 11, and it is possible to obtain this as we show later. So the best bound is dd where 1+3+5+…+(2⋅d−1)≤n1+3+5+…+(2⋅d−1)≤n, which comes out to be n−−√n.

Construction

Let L=n−−√L=n. Make P1,P2,…,PLP1,P2,…,PL where PiPi is a line graph of size 2⋅i−12⋅i−1. Now, connect the central nodes of PiPi and Pi−1Pi−1 for all ii. Refer to Wuhudsm comment for a picture.

2107E - Ain and Apple Tree

Solution

Let C(n,k)C(n,k) denote n!k!⋅(n−k)!n!k!⋅(n−k)!.

We first start from the maximum possible value of kk, and the tree that achieves it, i.e. a straight line path rooted at 11. It is not too hard to see that this tree has the largest weight, and the weight of this tree is C(n,3)C(n,3). Thus, for k>C(n,3)+1k>C(n,3)+1, we answer NoNo. We can prove the largest weight point by proving each node should have exactly 11 child. Suppose instead some node uu has ≥2≥2 children, c1c1 and c2c2, but then if we attached c1c1 to c2c2 and broke the c1c1 and uu edge, it increases the weight.

Now, infact the answer is possible for all 0≤k≤C(n,3)+10≤k≤C(n,3)+1. We try to modify the line graph to give a valid construction. First of all, replace kk with C(n,3)−kC(n,3)−k and view the problem as reducing the weight of the line graph instead.

Suppose we broke the edge (n−1)−n(n−1)−n and added the edge x−nx−n, then the weight decreases by C(n−x,2)C(n−x,2). This is because the depth of LCA with nodes n−1n−1, n−2,…,xn−2,…,x change and they decrease by n−x−1,n−x−2,…1n−x−1,n−x−2,…1 respectively. Suppose, after this step, we further broke the edge (n−2)−(n−1)(n−2)−(n−1), and added the edge y−(n−1)y−(n−1). It is tempting to say that the weight again decreases by C(n−1−y,2)C(n−1−y,2) but this is only true when y≥xy≥x.

If we generalize the above, and break the edges (i−1)−i(i−1)−i and add the edge ai−iai−i (ai<iai<i), then the weight will decrease by C(i−ai,2)C(i−ai,2) as long as the sequence aa is decreasing (or ai=i−1ai=i−1, i.e. we do not change the parent). Because aa must be decreasing, that means that i−aii−ai can only contain distinct elements (or i−ai=1i−ai=1).

This reduces the problem to Find sum of distinct elements of C(i,2)C(i,2) (0≤i≤n−10≤i≤n−1)at most 11 from k.

Infact the greedy algorithm solves this problem, i.e. take the largest (untaken) value of C(i,2)≤kC(i,2)≤k at each step.

Proof

We prove by induction. For n≤5n≤5, it can be verified through bruteforce for all cases.

For n≥6n≥6, if kk is ≤C(n−1,3)≤C(n−1,3), just set n=n−1n=n−1 and by induction it is solvable. If C(n−1,3)<k≤C(n,3)C(n−1,3)<k≤C(n,3), then we set k=k−C(n−1,2)k=k−C(n−1,2) and use induction. It can be verified that 0≤k≤C(n−1,3)0≤k≤C(n−1,3) after this modification and so it is valid.

Code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--){
        int n; cin >> n;
        long long k; cin >> k;
        
        long long mx = 1LL * n * (n - 1) * (n - 2) / 6;
        if (k > mx + 1){
            cout << "No\n";
            continue;
        }
        cout << "Yes\n";
        k = mx - min(k, mx);
        
        int p = n - 1;
        for (int i = n; i >= 2; i--){
            while (1LL * p * (p - 1) / 2 > k){
                p--;
            }
            k -= 1LL * p * (p - 1) / 2;
            
            cout << (i - p) << " " << i << "\n";
            if (p != 1) p--;
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

2107F1 - Cycling (Easy Version)

Solution

Let us try to figure out the optimal strategy. We want to use small values for the "overtake" operation, and we will use "swap" operations to make the overtakes less costly.

Suppose p=p= first position of minima element. We should over take cyclists 1,2,…,p1,2,…,p for the cost of apap, swapping pp till first. This is because all ai(1≤i<p)ai(1≤i<p) is ≥ap+1≥ap+1, and so the swap + overtake can't be worse. And obviously, if we are using aq(q>p)aq(q>p), it is less costly in terms of swaps to use pp since the overtake cost cannot be worse.

Now, we might use the value apap for overtakes in the suffix as well, i.e. there exists some q≥pq≥p such that we overtake cyclists 1,2,…,q1,2,…,q using apap. This reduces the problem to the subproblem a[q+1,n]a[q+1,n], and thus we can use Dynamic Programming! But why are the problems independent in a[q+1,n]a[q+1,n] and the rest of the array? Suppose that we took some ai(i≤q)ai(i≤q) to be used for swaps in the suffix a[q+1,n]a[q+1,n], but we could then instead set up the swaps such that first pp reaches qq, and then pp is taken for these suffix swaps. This won't add any additional swaps, and may only reduce the cost.

Let dpidpi be the answer for the suffix a[i,n]a[i,n]. Then, we compute p=p= position of minima in a[i,n]a[i,n], iterate on partition index jj such that we will take pp to jj using it for swaps 1,2…,j1,2…,j, value computed as dpj+1+ap⋅(j−i)+Sdpj+1+ap⋅(j−i)+S, where SS denotes the total swaps made (S=2⋅(j−p)+(p−i−1)S=2⋅(j−p)+(p−i−1)).

This solves the problem in O(n2)O(n2).

Code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--){
        int n; cin >> n;
        
        vector <int> a(n + 1);
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
            cin >> a[i];
        }
        
        vector <long long> dp(n + 1, 1e18);
        dp[n] = 0;
        
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--){
            int p = i + 1;
            for (int j = i + 1; j <= n; j++) if (a[j] < a[p]){
                p = j;
            }
            
            for (int j = p; j <= n; j++){
                dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[j] + 2 * (j - p) + 1LL * (j - i) * a[p] + (p - i - 1));
            }
        }
        
        cout << dp[0] << "\n";
    }
    return 0;
}

2107F2 - Cycling (Hard Version)

Solution

Read the editorial for F1 first. We considered the positions that we would take pp to, which we assumed was qq and iterated q=p,p+1,…,nq=p,p+1,…,n, finding the cost for each.

Property : Optimal qq is only q=pq=p or q=nq=n.

Proof

Suppose q≠pq≠p, i.e. we use apap for overtaking p+1p+1, and we used aiai for overtaking q+1q+1. Then, if ap+2≤aiap+2≤ai, it is not less optimal to overtake q+1q+1 using apap as well, and if ai≤ap+1ai≤ap+1, it is not less optimal to overtake qq using aiai.

Thus, qq is only ever the "edges" of the ranges , i.e. pp or nn.

Now, let p1p1 be the first minima in a[1,n]a[1,n], then p2p2 be the first minima in a[p1+1,n]a[p1+1,n], p3p3 be the first minima in a[p2+1,n]a[p2+1,n], and so on. Let qi=qi= where pipi is taken to for each ii. Due to the above property, we can see that the optimal strategy is : for some xx, qx=nqx=n and qi=piqi=pi for i<xi<x, i.e. choose to take some index pxpx to nn, and the previous indices do not move.

We get a O(n)O(n) solution for a single array by trying all values of xx. But, we need to answer prefix queries.

Note that each option of xx gives a linear equation in the length of the array. And appending an element to the end of the array adds at most 11 extra option for xx. We can maintain all the linear equations in a CHT/Lichao tree and find the minimum each time.

Extra

It can be proven that only min(a)≤ai≤min(a)+20min(a)≤ai≤min(a)+20 need to be considered, and we will never overtake using a larger value. This property gives us simpler solutions in O(n⋅20)O(n⋅20) with 00 data structures.

Proof

Assume min(a)=1min(a)=1. Let LiLi denote the number of overtakes using the value ii, and we use values 1,2…,k1,2…,k. Then, it is easy to see the indices 1,2,…,L11,2,…,L1 will be overtaken with 11, after that L1+1,L1+2,…,L2L1+1,L1+2,…,L2 will be overtaken with 22 and so on.

It should not be more optimal to take the value ii to cover the entire suffix, and so this gives us additional constraints of the form (by calculating the difference of costs in the current way, and the modified way) 2⋅(Li+1+Li+2+…+Lk)>Li+1⋅1+Li+2⋅2+Li+3⋅3+…2⋅(Li+1+Li+2+…+Lk)>Li+1⋅1+Li+2⋅2+Li+3⋅3+…. This simplies to Li+1≥Li+3+2⋅Li+4+…Li+1≥Li+3+2⋅Li+4+….

Because of Li+1≥2⋅Li+4Li+1≥2⋅Li+4, we get a bound of 3⋅log2(n)3⋅log2(n), but the exact bound can be computed by a greedy approach.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long
#define INF (int)1e18

#define inf 1e18
#define ld long double

mt19937_64 RNG(chrono::steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());

 
struct chtDynamicMin {  
	struct line {
		int m, b; ld x; 
		int om, ob;
		int val; bool isQuery; 
		line(int _m = 0, int _b = 0, int _om = 0, int _ob = 0) {
			m = _m;
			b = _b;
			om = _om;
			ob = _ob;
			val = 0;
			x = -inf;
			isQuery = false;
		}
			
		
		int eval(int x) const { return m * x + b;	}
		bool parallel(const line &l) const { return m == l.m; }
		ld intersect(const line &l) const {
			return parallel(l) ? inf : 1.0 * (l.b - b) / (m - l.m);
		}
		bool operator < (const line &l) const {
			if(l.isQuery) return x < l.val;
			else return m < l.m; 
		}
	};
 
	set<line> hull; 
	typedef set<line> :: iterator iter; 
 
	bool cPrev(iter it) { return it != hull.begin(); }
	bool cNext(iter it) { return it != hull.end() && next(it) != hull.end(); }
 
	bool bad(const line &l1, const line &l2, const line &l3) {
		return l1.intersect(l3) <= l1.intersect(l2); 
	}
	bool bad(iter it) {
		return cPrev(it) && cNext(it) && bad(*prev(it), *it, *next(it));
	}
 
	iter update(iter it) {
		if(!cPrev(it)) return it; 
		ld x = it -> intersect(*prev(it));
		line tmp(*it); tmp.x = x;
		it = hull.erase(it); 
		return hull.insert(it, tmp);
	}
    
	void addLine(int m, int b) { 
		int om = m, ob = b;
		m *= -1;
		b *= -1;
		line l(m, b, om, ob); 
		iter it = hull.lower_bound(l); 
		if(it != hull.end() && l.parallel(*it)) {
			if(it -> b < b) it = hull.erase(it); 
			else return;
		}
 
		it = hull.insert(it, l); 
		if(bad(it)) return (void) hull.erase(it);
 
		while(cPrev(it) && bad(prev(it))) hull.erase(prev(it));
		while(cNext(it) && bad(next(it))) hull.erase(next(it));
 
		it = update(it);
		if(cPrev(it)) update(prev(it));
		if(cNext(it)) update(next(it));
	}
 
	int query(int x) const { 
		if(hull.empty()) return inf;
		line q; q.val = x, q.isQuery = 1;
		iter it = --hull.lower_bound(q);
		return - it -> eval(x);
	}
};

void Solve() 
{
    int n; cin >> n;
    
    // insert lines and calculate CHT 
    chtDynamicMin cht;
    
    vector <int> a(n + 1);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        cin >> a[i];
    }
    
    vector <int> pv(n + 1, 0);
    // previous smaller 
    stack <pair<int, int>> st;
    for (int i = n; i >= 1; i--){
        while (!st.empty() && st.top().first >= a[i]){
            pv[st.top().second] = i;
            st.pop();
        }
        
        st.push({a[i], i});
    }
    
    vector <int> dp(n + 1, 0);
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        dp[i] = dp[pv[i]] + a[i] * (i - pv[i]) + (i - pv[i] - 1);
        // slope is +2 + a[i] 
        // constant - slope * i  
        int m = 2 + a[i];
        int c = dp[i] - m * i;
        cht.addLine(m, c);
        
        int ans = cht.query(i);
        cout << ans << " \n"[i == n];
    }
}

int32_t main() 
{
    auto begin = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t = 1;
    // freopen("in",  "r", stdin);
    // freopen("out", "w", stdout);
    
    cin >> t;
    for(int i = 1; i <= t; i++) 
    {
        //cout << "Case #" << i << ": ";
        Solve();
    }
    auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto elapsed = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::nanoseconds>(end - begin);
    cerr << "Time measured: " << elapsed.count() * 1e-9 << " seconds.\n"; 
    return 0;
}
Tutorial of Codeforces Round 1023 (Div. 2)

»
joaozao
11 months ago, show (+1) #
»
joaozao
11 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+16

Problem F1 can be solved greedily.

First, I reversed the array for simplicity.

Now, assume that the value in the first position is fixed. We can see that we can take this first element and swap it with the second one at a cost of 11. Then, again, we can take the second element and swap it with the third one, also at a cost of 11, and so on.

This is helpful because we can carry the smallest element seen so far and use it whenever we want, and the cost to use it is its value plus 11.

So, at each position, we just have to choose whether to use its current value (which is a[i], and then update the current minimum value to a[i], so it can be used in the next iteration), or simply use the minimum value carried all the way to this point plus 11.

To do this, we initially fixed the first element. But since N≤5⋅103N≤5⋅103, we can simply iterate over all elements, assuming each one as the potential first element, solve the problem under that assumption, and then take the minimum among all of these.

Code: https://codeforces.com/contest/2107/submission/318519641

→ Reply

  • »

    +5

    How does your tree DP work for problem D?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      TimurKul
      11 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      TimurKul
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      Submission link: https://codeforces.com/contest/2107/submission/318526303

      Let node 1 be the root of the tree. For every node we store the farthest child (if there is a tie, then the child with the maximum label) and the lexicographically greatest possible (d, u, v) in the subtree where the current node is the root. Then we begin traversing the tree from the root.

      If current node is a part of the path (u, v), then it's sufficient to remove every node from u to v and process remaining subtrees, otherwise we process the subtree that contains the path (u, v), update every (not removed yet) ancestor of the root of the processed subtree and repeat the described algorithm in the current node again.

      Apologies for the poor description, but this is probably the most horrible solution available for this problem and it's even more painful trying to explain it :)

      → Reply

I'm so confused why my solution to problem A did not work

I got, "wrong answer the gcds of A,B are equal. (test case 59)"

How can it be equal? I designed it in such a way that

  • sequence BB only has one number axax that is the second smallest number in the original aa,
  • then put the the rest of elements of aa in sequence AA

I sorted array a, then I conjectured that this is enough

This means that array aa is sorted in such a way that

  • a1,a2,a3,...,ax,ax+1,...,an−1,ana1,a2,a3,...,ax,ax+1,...,an−1,an,
  • where a1=a2=a3,...a1=a2=a3,... (the first few elements may be smallest)
  • ...,a3<ax,......,a3<ax,... (but axax is guaranteed to be second smallest)
  • ax≤ax+1,ax+1≤an−1,...,an−1≤anax≤ax+1,ax+1≤an−1,...,an−1≤an

I then put axax into sequence BB. There should be no way gcd(ax)gcd(ax) equal gcd(a1,a2,a3,...,ax+1,...,an−1,an)gcd(a1,a2,a3,...,ax+1,...,an−1,an)?

But there can be multiple other reasons: for example, if you accidentally take part in a contest you tested, we will skip your submissions. Or if you submit to the same problem multiple times after AC, your first submit will be skipped (there are non cheating reasons to do this)

          This is a case of the last type

in problem D. lets say the diameter of the tree is 19 from u to v and that would be (19,u,v). then there would exist a path of length 9 from x to y so (9,x,y). if i understand the solution correctly we pick the (19,u,v) first but isnt (9,x,y) lexicograpically the larger one? first stars with 1 and the second starts with 9.

    Erering
    11 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Erering
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    array {19} is lexicographically larger than {9}. This isnt a string but an array of numbers so definition is based on the number and not the digits.

arvindf232
11 months ago, show (+1) #
»
arvindf232
11 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 4 →  
+20

No thinking, raw CHT solution for F2.(everything 0 indexed)

same as the editorial, we start with dp[i] for i in 0..n, and we would like the make transition of the form: for some i<=k<j , we use element L[k] to cover the range i..j. -1. If v=L[i], the cost of this is v*(j-i) + (j-1-k) + (j-1-i). Unlike in the editorial, we do not reduce the number of transitions to consider away from n^3, but just do it.

thinking about this in another way, we are paying an additional cost of (v+1) for every element before L[k] and an additional(v+2) for every element after L[k].

We can imagine this as a 2-stage DP, where each element k pulls from before and push to after. Each of these can be handled with an add-only CHT (aka line container). Li Chao tree is also applicable here and is faster, but the constraints are pretty lenient. We need two different CHT/ Li Chao Tree, one for each component of the transition.

Complexity is n log n. You can check my submission for detailed calculations. (Idea took me 5 minutes but getting the formula right took me the remaining 20….)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    DNR
    6 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    DNR
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    Just adding a hopefully clearer write-up of this solution here.

    Observation 1: We can partition [1,n][1,n] into several maximal non-intersecting ranges such that for each range [l,r][l,r] we use only element ayay (where l−1≤y≤rl−1≤y≤r), and no two ranges use the same element.

    Proof

    Exercise for the reader. It's easy to prove via contradiction.

    Observation 2: The contribution of each such triple [l,r,y][l,r,y] to the cost is (r−y)+(ay+1)⋅(r−l+1)−1(r−y)+(ay+1)⋅(r−l+1)−1.

    Proof

    We swap (y,r)(y,r), then take arar, swap (r−1,r)(r−1,r), take ar−1ar−1, swap (r−2,r−1)(r−2,r−1)...

    We will use dynamic programming to find the optimal partition for each prefix.

    Definitions:

    • f(i,0)f(i,0) is the minimum cost for partitioning the prefix ii such that no element from this prefix can be taken by some segment ahead (note that this only happens when we use element aiai for the last segment in [1,i][1,i]).
    • f(i,1)f(i,1) is the minimum cost for partitioning the prefix ii such that only element aiai from this prefix can be taken by some segment ahead.

    Transitions (evaluated in increasing order of ii):

    • f(i,0)=minj<i(min(f(j,0),f(j,1))+(ai+1)⋅(i−j)−1)f(i,0)=minj<i(min(f(j,0),f(j,1))+(ai+1)⋅(i−j)−1)
    • f(i,1)=minj<i(min(f(j,0)+1,f(j,1))+(i−j)⋅(aj+2)−1)f(i,1)=minj<i(min(f(j,0)+1,f(j,1))+(i−j)⋅(aj+2)−1)

    Both transitions can be performed using two cht's.

    The correctness of the first transition is fairly obvious, but what about that of the second? Notice that if use y<ry<r for some segment [l,r][l,r], and we've already handled the cost on [r,y][r,y] (which is done here through the term min(f(j,0)+1,f(j,1))min(f(j,0)+1,f(j,1))), then the cost on (y,l](y,l] is simply ay+2ay+2 per element (11 for the initial swap (y,r)(y,r), 11 for the swap (i,i+1)(i,i+1), and ayay for simply taking that element). The exception here is the last element, which only has a cost of ay+1ay+1 as it doesn't require a (i,i+1)(i,i+1) swap. So the total cost for (y,r](y,r] is (r−y)⋅(ay+2)−1(r−y)⋅(ay+2)−1.

    The answer for prefix ii is obviously min(f(i,0),f(i,1))min(f(i,0),f(i,1)).

    Code: 342809058

    → Reply
»

In the editorial for D, I'm not sure how property 3 is coming into play here. The property holds when all aiai are distinct, but in this case, when we remove a diameter, the tree is split into a forest, and many components in that forest might have the same diameter (it would be smaller for sure, but repetition is possible). So we can't say that all aiai are distinct right?

Edit: Understood now, we are looking from the perspective of each node, a single node won't be involved in more than O((√n))O((n)) operations

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Flash-
    11 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Flash-
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Could explain how looking from the perspective of each node makes sure that there is O(sqrt(n)) subtrees?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      yellow_13
      10 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      yellow_13
      10 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      So in the end every single node will be disconnected right. Now for each node, let us focus only on operations which affected the component in which that node belongs. Since you're only focusing the operations performed in a particular component, the diameter will be strictly decreasing, hence as mentioned in the editorial, there can only be O(n−−√)O(n) operations that involve the connected component of that node, hence if we add this over all nodes, the total number of operations is O(nn−−√)O(nn)

      → Reply

F2 with lichaotree O(nlogn)

318618090

I have a same idea with author but with different explanation,so you think tutorial is a little bit hard to understand then you can give this comment a try

First,let us forget for range [1,i] and i∊[1,n],we consider for range [1,n],now we observe something let us find the first minimum(minimum element with minimum index),let say its index is P1,now obviously for i<P1 swap a[P1] to i<P1 is better because for i<P1 a[i]>a[P1] this imply a[P1]+1<=a[i] now we make another observation is does a[P1] just left at P1 or we can move this to the back in otherword move a[p1] to j for j∊[p1+1,n]?The author claim if and only if p1 move backward there only 1 place which is n,the logic is quite simple

Proof for a[pi] left at pi or n

Assume that there exist a number k which k>p1 and k<n,and we move a[p1] to k,now we think about a[k+1],if a[p1]+2<=a[k+1] then we should move a[p1] to k+1,so we repeat this operation until there is one point q,q>p1 && q<n && a[p1]+2>a[q],if a[p1]+2>a[q] this imply a[q]<=a[p1]+1,then we assume that a[q] with the maximum which is a[p1]+1 then from q to p1 the element is count as a[p1]+2 and a[p1]+1 for q in otherword for[p1+1,q) we count as a[q]+1=max(a[q])+1=a[p1]+2,and for[q,q] we count as a[p1]+1,and if we move a[p1] to q-1 then [p1+1,q) we also count as a[p1]+2,so this mean if exist one q such that q>p1 && q<n we should not move p1 to backward just let him stay at the original place or we can't find such q this imply we move p1 to n,so a[pi] either stay at pi or n



Now assume that for range [1,n] we can find all pi like this first find the minimum element with minimum index in [1,n] ,let says p1 and find p2 in [p1+1,n] and so on pi in [pi-1,n],the reason is if a[p1] stay at p1 then we can use p2 and so on,so we need to check is there exist an x such that a[px] move to n and for i<x a[p[i]] stay at original place.

At the last,how to implement it?We iterate from i∊[1,n] and assume that our range is [1,k] which is we are initially located behind a[k] and want to go in front of a[1],now we assume for range[1,k] the satisfy the criterion of index is px in otherword for range[1,k] assume that there is there m element of pi and px(x<=m) now how the answer look like?answer should be (cost of move a[px] to k + a[px]*how many element between k and px (px,k] + dp[px]),we define dp[i] is the minimum cost for behind index i to in front of 1,so answer=2*(k-px)+a[px]*(k-px)+dp[px],we can rewrite the equation 2*k+a[px]*k-2*px-a[px]*px+dp[px]=k*(2+a[px])+dp[px]-(2+a[px])*px,you may ask how to calculate dp[px]?when you iterate from 1 to n you can assume that current i is px for range and calculate dp[px]=from px to first element <= a[px] which is dp[px]=a[px]*(px-left[px])+(px-left[px]-1)+dp[left[px]],you can let x=k,m=2+a[px] and c=dp[px]-(2+a[px])*px,you will find answer=mx+c,this is a line,and reading until here you should notice that this is a convex hull trick problem/lichao tree problem,you can find the tutorial of lichao tree everywhere,i just conclude the key,lichao tree allow us to insert line as the range and the basic logic of lichao tree is segment tree it maintain the minimum/maximum line for range,so according to the previous result the answer is the min lichao tree for query k,

→ Reply

    You find diameters in O(n)O(n) by DFS run

    • »
      »

      wassup69
      11 months ago, show (+2) #
      »

      »
      wassup69
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +3

      If we find diameters in O(n) for each component in the forest, how are we saying it is bounded by n sqrt(n)?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        ashish1729
        11 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        ashish1729
        11 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +9

        the point is that tree size reduces in each step, the proof in editorial explains that this reduction can't take more than sqrt N steps...

        so yes you do O(n) to find the diameter but only sqrt N times.

        Imagine like layers of merge sort... each layer altogether takes O(n) time but there are only logN layers.

        → Reply

  • »

    people_plus_plus
    11 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    people_plus_plus
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I used this approach:

    1. Let didi be equal to answer on prefix [1,n][1,n]. Base is d0=0d0=0.

    2. Now, we can move from ii to another index jj (j<i)(j<i) by using aiai, we always jump using aiai, so we move from ii to i−1i−1 paying aiai, then we swaps (ai,ai−1)(ai,ai−1) and so on, until we arrive at position jj. Formula will look as (j−i)⋅ai+j−i(j−i)⋅ai+j−i or something like that. Just iteratate over all possible jj in O(n)O(n).

    3. And we can use another number jj (j<i)(j<i), swap (ai,aj)(ai,aj) and move from ii to jj using ajaj at each jump. Formula will look as 2(j−i)⋅aj+2(j−i)2(j−i)⋅aj+2(j−i) or something like that. Just iteratate over all possible jj in O(n)O(n).

    → Reply

Hi @ everyone ,For C The author mentioned this in the editorial.

Thus, we can simply replace x with k−b−c and it will satisfy the conditions.

Can anyone tell me , why this line will be true in case if the sum of second subarray which is prefix array or the right subarray after the position pos is negative (because it can since the values which I replace with is -INF and also the input elements can be negative too ) then the sum of b + c will be lesser than b in case if b is positive , due to which i get bigger value of x = k — b — c which then if I add with the b; b + x will be > k it will exceeds k

why that is not being handled why we are without checking just assigning x = k — (b + c) {sum of two portions} , I have solved by my own but included the case if b + c >= k then we can use it, othwerwise I have to take the max of b , c and (b+c) due to the possibility of negative sum which can reduce the overall combined sum.

Why this code got Accepted in A? I think it will TLE...

My code:

#include<bits/stdc++.h>
#define int long long
#define inf (1ll << 62)
#define regint register int
#define pb push_back
#define mp make_pair
#define PII pair<int , int>
using namespace std;
const int MAXN = 105 , MAXM = 1e4 + 10;
int t , n;
int a[MAXN];

inline void solve() {
	cin >> n;
	int t = - inf;
	vector<int>G[MAXM];
	for(regint i = 1;i <= n;i ++) {
		cin >> a[i];
		for(regint j = 1;j <= sqrt(a[i]);j ++) {
			if(a[i] % j == 0) {
				G[j].pb(i);
				if(j != a[i] / j) {
					G[a[i] / j].pb(i);	
				}
				t = max(t , a[i] / j);
			}
		}
 	}
 	bool vis = false;
 	for(regint i = 1;i <= t;i ++) {
		if(!G[i].size() || G[i].size() == n) {
			continue;
		}
		int g1 = -1 , g2 = -1;
		vector<bool>flag(n + 1);
		for(auto j : G[i]) {
			if(g1 == -1) {
				g1 = a[j];
			} else {
				g1 = __gcd(g1 , a[j]);
			}
			flag[j] = true;
		}
		for(regint j = 1;j <= n;j ++) {
			if(!flag[j]) {
				if(g2 == -1) {
					g2 = a[j];
				} else {
					g2 = __gcd(g2 , a[j]);
				}
			}
		}
		if(g1 != g2) {
			cout << "Yes" << '\n';
			vis = true;
			for(regint i = 1;i <= n;i ++) {
				if(flag[i]) {
					cout << 1 << " ";
				} else {
					cout << 2 << " ";
				}
			}
			cout << '\n';
			break;
		}
	}
	if(!vis) {
		cout << "No" << '\n';
	}
	return;
}

signed main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
	cin >> t;
	while(t --) {
		solve();
	}
	return 0;
}
→ Reply

Iwakura_Rein
10 months ago, show #

Iwakura_Rein
10 months ago, hide # |
 
0

O(nlogn) solution for D: (implementation details heavily rely on sets with a custom comparison struct)

let 1 be the root of the tree then for every path, there will be 1 node in it with the least depth (distance to 1)

we first traverse the entire graph by a dfs, the lexi-greatest path with node x as its root node (node with least depth), will be its 2 lexi-greatest branches, connected with itself.

  1. we can obtain the lexi-greatest path of all nodes with a single dfs from 1 (O(n)) I also stored a childs set in each node, in lexi-order (O(nlogn) for all nodes)

  2. insert all nodes in a set, that compares the lexi-value of the longest path of each node (O(nlogn))

  3. then, remove the largest valued node in the set (this could be first or last depending on your ordering) remove all of the child nodes on this path) (O(klogn), k being the nodes removed)

  4. notice, after removing these nodes, only the parents of the largest valued node would be impacted after the removal of these nodes, for nodes on other branches, the highest lexi-value path with them as the root node stays the same

and there are a maximum of k parent nodes, otherwise their would be a longer path than the removed one, so modifying these nodes also take O(klogn) time

  1. return to step 3, until all nodes are removed since the sum of k's is n, these last steps take a total of O(nlogn) time

here is my submission, my implementation skills on graph problems are **** but it shows the method works319444417

→ Reply

For the B problem, consider the case 2 boxes, k=1 and a1,a2=6,6. Tom plays first ->5,6. Jerry doesn't have any other option but to equalise or will lose due to the max-min<=k constraint-> 5,5. This continues and tom will end up winning. This is irrespective of summation of 'a' being even or odd. In this case summation is even and still tom wins. Please help where did i go wrong.

      What if we have the max element more than one time, then we should compare with

      max_element-min_element > k

      Instead of max_element-min_element-1 > k

      For e.g

      4 2

      7 7 4 5

      Here Tom loses, but still winner according to solution?

      Am I missing something?

      → Reply

        difficult_saurabh
        6 months ago, show #

        difficult_saurabh
        6 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        In your example, after the first move (Tom chosing to reduce first occurence of 7 to 6) the array becomes a = [ 6, 7, 4, 5 ] after the move, d = max(a) = min(a) = 3, d > k follows --> Tom loses! According to the solution as well, Tom does lose.

        → Reply

0

In div2C, isn't it enough to limit INF to k=10^12? I'm not sure why we need to keep a_i terms in sum, as largest consecutive block will be at most 10^12 and summed with -INF, it will cancel out.

One simple way is to have INF=10^13 , as that is sufficient for our purposes (prevents overflow, but still larger than k+∑aik+∑ai).

→ Reply
